title: Getting Started with Quarto
text:
NoteOfficial Documentation



The official Quarto documentation at quarto.org/docs/get-started contains detailed tutorials for all skill levels — always a great place to look for more detail.
Welcome! This guide walks you through creating real Quarto documents — step by step, like a building manual. No prior experience needed.

title: Getting Started with Quarto
section: What would you like to build?
text:
What would you like to build?


🌐 A Website
Present your course, research, or portfolio as a clean multi-page website.
→ What you need → Create a website → Publish online


📄 An Exercise Sheet
Create a professional exercise sheet that renders to PDF, HTML, or Word from one file.
→ What you need → Create a sheet → Other formats


📚 A Book
Structure longer content — lecture notes, a handbook — as a navigable book.
→ What you need → Create a book

title: Getting Started with Quarto
text:
✨ Want interactive Python code?
If you want students to run and edit Python code directly in the browser (no installation needed for them), check out the Extensions guide.

title: Create a Website
text:
Create a new empty folder on your computer (e.g. on the Desktop). Name it something short without spaces, like my-website.
In VS Code click File → Open Folder and select your new folder.






WarningVS Code will ask: “Do you trust the authors?”




This dialog appears the first time you open any folder. Click Yes, I trust the authors — otherwise VS Code runs in a restricted mode and some features will not work.

title: Create a Website
text:
Create a new empty folder on your computer (e.g. on the Desktop). Name it something short without spaces, like my-website.
In VS Code click File → Open Folder and select your new folder.






WarningVS Code will ask: “Do you trust the authors?”




This dialog appears the first time you open any folder. Click Yes, I trust the authors — otherwise VS Code runs in a restricted mode and some features will not work.

title: Create a Website
text:
2 — Create a Quarto Website project
① Open the Command Palette and create a project

Press Ctrl+Shift+P, type Quarto: Create Project and press Enter. A menu appears — select Website Project.

② Your project files are ready

Quarto created four files:

_quarto.yml — settings: title, navigation, theme
index.qmd — your homepage
about.qmd — an example second page
styles.css — optional custom styling

title: Create a Website
text:
3 — Preview your website
① Click the Preview button in the top-right corner of the editor

Click the small preview icon in the top-right corner of the coding window. VS Code opens the terminal automatically, renders your site, and shows a live preview in a new panel on the right — no browser needed.







Note🔍 Other ways to preview and render





Render files (create output without previewing)
If you just want to produce the output files without opening any preview, open the terminal (View → Terminal) and run:
quarto render
This creates all output files in the _site folder (or docs/ if you configured it that way). Use this when you are ready to publish — see Publish your Website →.

Preview in your browser
You have two options:

Open the generated HTML files from the _site folder directly in your browser (double-click them in File Explorer).
Or run quarto preview in the terminal — this starts a local server and opens your site in the browser automatically. The browser updates every time you save a file.












Tip✅ Your first Quarto website is running!



Next steps:

Edit index.qmd and save — the preview updates immediately
Change the title: in _quarto.yml to rename your site
Ready to go online? Continue with Publish your Website →

title: Interactive Code with Pyodide
text:
NoteWhat are extensions?



Extensions add extra features to Quarto that are not built in. You install them once per project folder. See the full list at quarto.org/docs/extensions.
Pyodide lets students run and edit Python code directly in the browser — no Python installation needed on their computer. You write the code once; they click Run Code.

title: Interactive Code with Pyodide
text:
1 — Install the Pyodide extension
① Open the terminal in VS Code
Go to View → Terminal. A panel opens at the bottom of the screen. Make sure you are in your project folder (the path shown in the terminal should match your project).

② Run the install command

Type the following and press Enter:
quarto add coatless-quarto/pyodide
Wait until it finishes — it will ask you to confirm once. This only needs to be done once per project folder.

title: Interactive Code with Pyodide
text:
2 — Enable it in your file
Add a filters: line to the header block of your .qmd file:
---
title: "My Page"
format: html
filters:
  - coatless-quarto/pyodide
---

title: Interactive Code with Pyodide
text:
3 — Add an interactive code block
Instead of a regular code block, use {pyodide-python}:
```{pyodide-python}
x = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
print(sum(x))
```
Students can edit this code directly in the browser and run it with the Run Code button.

title: Interactive Code with Pyodide
text:
4 — See the result
① Preview your page
Run quarto preview in the terminal. The code block appears in the browser with a Run Code button.

② The interactive block in the browser

At the top of the page Quarto shows PYODIDE STATUS: Ready! when Python has loaded. Students can click Run Code, edit the code, and run it again — all without installing anything.







TipWant students to fill in solutions?



The py-exercise extension adds code blocks with hidden automated tests — students see whether their solution passes or fails. You can see a live example on the Example Page of this tutorial.

title: Create an Exercise Sheet
text:
① Open the New File dialog

In VS Code press Ctrl+Alt+Windows+N — or go to File → New File…. A small menu appears. Select Quarto Document.

② Your file opens with a starter header

VS Code creates a new file with a basic settings block already filled in. Save it with a name like exercises.qmd (Ctrl+S).
crumbs: Creating an Exercise Sheet with Quarto

title: Create an Exercise Sheet
text:
① Open the New File dialog

In VS Code press Ctrl+Alt+Windows+N — or go to File → New File…. A small menu appears. Select Quarto Document.

② Your file opens with a starter header

VS Code creates a new file with a basic settings block already filled in. Save it with a name like exercises.qmd (Ctrl+S).
crumbs: Creating an Exercise Sheet with Quarto

title: Create an Exercise Sheet
text:
2 — Set the output format
① Edit the header block
The top of the file controls the output format. Change it to:
---
title: "Exercise Sheet 1"
author: "Your Name"
format: pdf
---
This tells Quarto: create a PDF with this title and author name.






Note



PDF output requires TinyTeX — see What you need if you have not installed it yet. For HTML output, change format: pdf to format: html and no extra tools are needed.
crumbs: Creating an Exercise Sheet with Quarto

title: Create an Exercise Sheet
text:
3 — Write your exercises
Below the header block, write your content using normal text. Use ## for section headings. Leave a blank line between paragraphs.
## Exercise 1

Calculate the derivative of $f(x) = x^2 + 3x$.

## Exercise 2

Explain the difference between a list and a dictionary in Python.
crumbs: Creating an Exercise Sheet with Quarto

title: Create an Exercise Sheet
text:
4 — Add a code example (optional)
To show Python code that is displayed but not executed:
```python
x = [1, 2, 3, 4]
print(sum(x))
```
crumbs: Creating an Exercise Sheet with Quarto

title: Create an Exercise Sheet
text:
5 — Render your sheet
Open the terminal (View → Terminal) and run:
quarto render exercises.qmd

Quarto creates the output file (PDF or HTML) in the same folder. Open it from your file explorer.






Note🔍 Preview while writing





Instead of quarto render, you can use:
quarto preview exercises.qmd
This opens a live preview in your browser that updates every time you save the file.










Tip✅ Your exercise sheet is ready!




Want to export as HTML, Word, or PDF from the same file? See Other Output Formats →
Want students to run and edit Python code in the browser? See Interactive Code →
crumbs: Creating an Exercise Sheet with Quarto

title: Create a Book
text: Create a new empty folder (e.g. my-book) and open it in VS Code via File → Open Folder. Click Yes, I trust the authors if the dialog appears.

title: Create a Book
text: Create a new empty folder (e.g. my-book) and open it in VS Code via File → Open Folder. Click Yes, I trust the authors if the dialog appears.

title: Create a Book
text:
2 — Create a Book project
① Open the Command Palette and select Book

Press Ctrl+Shift+P, type Quarto: Create Project and press Enter. Select Book Project from the list.

② Your book files are ready

Quarto created the full book structure:

_quarto.yml — book settings and chapter order
index.qmd — cover / preface
intro.qmd, summary.qmd — example chapters
references.bib — bibliography file
references.qmd — references page

title: Create a Book
text:
3 — Preview your book
① Run quarto preview in the terminal

Open the terminal (View → Terminal) and run:
quarto preview
The book builds and a live preview opens in VS Code and in your browser.

② See the result in your browser

Your book opens in the browser with:

A left sidebar listing all chapters
Previous / Next navigation at the bottom of each page
A table of contents on the right for the current chapter

title: Create a Book
text:
4 — Add and edit chapters
Open _quarto.yml to control which files are chapters and in what order:
book:
  title: "My Lecture Notes"
  author: "Your Name"
  chapters:
    - index.qmd
    - intro.qmd
    - summary.qmd
    - references.qmd
Each entry is the filename of a .qmd file in your folder. Add a new line to add a new chapter — create the matching .qmd file alongside it.







Tip✅ Your book is running!




Edit any chapter .qmd file and save — the preview updates instantly
Ready to publish? See Publish your Website → — the _book folder works exactly like the _site folder

title: Quarto Tutorial
text:
This is a Tutorial for using Quarto in a creating context.
If you never saw a Quarto page before click here!
If you want to learn how to create something with Quarto click here! If you already know how to create Quarto Documents and want to become better click here!

title: What you need — Book
text:
NoteOfficial Documentation



See the full Quarto book guide at quarto.org/docs/books.
A Quarto Book is a set of .qmd files that are rendered together into a navigable document — perfect for lecture notes, a course handbook, or documentation.
You need the same setup as for a website. If you have not done this yet, follow What you need — Website first.

title: What you need — Book
text:
How a book is structured
When you create a Book project, Quarto generates this file structure automatically:


_quarto.yml — defines the title, author, and the order of chapters
index.qmd — the cover / preface page
intro.qmd, summary.qmd — example chapters (you rename or replace these)
references.bib — bibliography file for citations
references.qmd — automatically generated references page

The _quarto.yml links everything together. For example:
book:
  title: "My Lecture Notes"
  author: "Your Name"
  chapters:
    - index.qmd
    - intro.qmd
    - summary.qmd
    - references.qmd
Each .qmd file becomes one chapter. The order in the list is the order in the book.







Tip✅ Ready!



Continue with Create a Book →

title: What you need — Exercise Sheet
text:
NoteOfficial Documentation



See quarto.org/docs/output-formats/pdf-basics.html for more about PDF output options.
To create an exercise sheet you need the same setup as for a website, plus one extra tool if you want PDF output.
crumbs: Creating an Exercise Sheet with Quarto

title: What you need — Exercise Sheet
text:
Step 1 — VS Code + Quarto extension
If you have not done this yet, follow What you need — Website and come back here when done.
crumbs: Creating an Exercise Sheet with Quarto

title: What you need — Exercise Sheet
section: Step 2 — Install TinyTeX (for PDF output only)
text:
Step 2 — Install TinyTeX (for PDF output only)
Quarto needs a LaTeX engine to create PDFs. The easiest option is TinyTeX, which Quarto can install for you directly from VS Code.
Open the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P), type Quarto: Install TinyTeX and press Enter. Wait for the installation to finish — this can take a few minutes.






Note



If you only want to produce HTML or Word output, you can skip this step entirely.









Tip✅ Ready!



Continue with Create an Exercise Sheet →
crumbs: Creating an Exercise Sheet with Quarto

title: Other Output Formats
text: One of Quarto’s biggest advantages: one .qmd file → multiple output formats. You write the content once and change only one line to get PDF, HTML, or Word.
crumbs: Creating an Exercise Sheet with Quarto

title: Other Output Formats
text:
Change the format
Open your .qmd file and edit the format: line in the header block at the top.
For HTML (no extra tools needed, works in any browser):
---
title: "Exercise Sheet 1"
format: html
---
For Word (.docx, can be edited in Microsoft Word):
---
title: "Exercise Sheet 1"
format: docx
---
For PDF (requires TinyTeX — see What you need):
---
title: "Exercise Sheet 1"
format: pdf
---
Then run quarto render in the terminal. Quarto produces the file in the format you chose.
crumbs: Creating an Exercise Sheet with Quarto

title: Other Output Formats
text:
Produce all formats at once
You can list multiple formats — Quarto will render all of them in one go:
---
title: "Exercise Sheet 1"
format:
  html: default
  pdf: default
  docx: default
---
Run quarto render once and you get three output files.







NoteOfficial Documentation



See all available formats and their options at quarto.org/docs/output-formats/all-formats.html.
crumbs: Creating an Exercise Sheet with Quarto

title: What you need — Website
text:
NoteOfficial Documentation



Full installation instructions are also available at quarto.org/docs/get-started.
You need three things installed. This takes about 10–15 minutes.

title: What you need — Website
text:
1 — Install VS Code
① Go to the VS Code website and download the installer

Go to code.visualstudio.com and click the large Download for Windows button. The file will download to your Downloads folder.

② Run the installer

Double-click the downloaded file (it looks like the VS Code logo with a green checkmark).

③ Make sure “Add to PATH” is checked

During installation you will see a screen with checkboxes. Make sure “Add to PATH” is ticked — this is important for Quarto to work correctly. Then click Next.

④ Finish the installation

Click Finish. VS Code will open automatically.






Note🔍 Detailed: every VS Code installer screen





License agreement — select “I accept” and click Next.

Destination folder — leave the default path and click Next.

Start menu folder — leave as is and click Next.

Summary screen — review and click Install.

Installation progress — wait for it to complete.

title: What you need — Website
text:
2 — Install Quarto
① Go to the Quarto website and download the installer

Go to quarto.org/docs/get-started and click the Download Quarto CLI button under Step 1. Choose the Windows installer.

② Run the Quarto installer

Double-click the downloaded .msi file and follow the installer. You can click through all screens with the default settings.

③ Finish

Click Finish. Quarto is now installed.






Note🔍 Detailed: every Quarto installer screen





Welcome screen — click Next.

Installation scope — “Install just for you” is fine for most users.

Destination folder — leave the default and click Next.

Installation progress — wait for it to complete.

title: What you need — Website
section: 3 — Install the Quarto Extension in VS Code
text:
3 — Install the Quarto Extension in VS Code
① Search for and install the Quarto extension

In VS Code, click the Extensions icon in the left sidebar (four squares). Search for Quarto, click on the result, and click Trust Publisher & Install in the dialog that appears.







Tip✅ All done — ready to build!



Continue with Create a Website →

title: Publish your Website
text:
NoteOfficial Documentation



Quarto supports several publishing options. See all of them at quarto.org/docs/publishing.
This guide uses GitHub Pages — a free hosting service that makes your website publicly available at a github.io address.
You will need a free account at github.com. Create one if you don’t have one yet.

title: Publish your Website
section: 1 — Tell Quarto to output into a docs folder
text:
1 — Tell Quarto to output into a docs folder
① Open _quarto.yml and add the output folder

Open your _quarto.yml file and add output-dir: docs to the project: section, exactly as shown above. GitHub Pages expects your website files to live in a folder named docs.

② Render your website
Open the terminal (View → Terminal) and run:
quarto render
Quarto builds your entire website into the docs/ folder. You will see it appear in the file explorer on the left.

title: Publish your Website
text:
2 — Create a repository on GitHub
① Go to GitHub and create a new repository

Log in at github.com, click the + icon in the top right and select New repository. Give it a name — this will become part of your website’s address.

② Set it to Public and create

Make sure Public is selected. Leave all other options at their defaults and click Create repository.

title: Publish your Website
text:
3 — Upload your files
① Find the upload link

Your new empty repository opens. Click uploading an existing file in the middle of the page.

② Drag your project files onto the page
Drag all files and folders from your project folder directly onto the GitHub upload page. Include everything: docs/, _quarto.yml, index.qmd, styles.css, and any other files.






Warning



Do not upload just the docs/ folder contents — upload the entire project folder’s contents so GitHub has everything it needs.


GitHub will show a “Processing your files…” spinner while uploading.


③ Commit the files

After uploading, scroll down. You will see a “Commit changes” dialog. You can leave the message as “Initial Push” or write your own. Click Commit changes.

title: Publish your Website
text:
4 — Enable GitHub Pages
① Open the repository Settings

Click the Settings tab at the top of your repository page.

② Find “Pages” in the left sidebar

Scroll down the left sidebar and click Pages.

③ Select branch main and folder /docs

Under Branch, click the dropdown that says None and select main.


A second dropdown appears for the folder. Click it and select /docs.

④ Save

Click Save. GitHub will now start building and deploying your site.

title: Publish your Website
text:
5 — Your site is live
① Wait for deployment

Go back to the main page of your repository. On the right side you will see a Deployments section. Wait until it shows a green checkmark — this usually takes 1–2 minutes.
Click on the deployment to find your public website address — it looks like https://yourusername.github.io/your-repo-name/.







TipUpdating your site later



Whenever you make changes:

Run quarto render in VS Code to rebuild the docs/ folder
Go to your GitHub repository → drag the updated docs/ folder onto the file list → Commit

GitHub Pages will redeploy automatically within a minute or two.
